fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: These mid-cap stocks have grown faster than large companies over the last 3 years (~23% yearly). We keep this smaller (20%) but it's where we're betting on wealth creation.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This fund manager picks large and mid-cap stocks together. Over 5 years it delivered ~20% returns. Flexi-cap means it can move its money into wherever stocks look cheapest.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Since you're in the new tax regime (no 80C deduction), we avoid ELSS. This 'large & mid' fund gives you exposure to both big stable companies and faster-growing smaller ones. 3-year returns ~21%.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Liquid Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Safe parking place (currently ~6% per year when placed wisely). This is your temporary home – if stocks correct down 30%, you slowly move money here instead of adding to panic. Over 32 years, debt will neutralise about 25% of your total risk.

plan.setup_phase: 1. Download the Groww or Coin app on your phone. Click 'Add Funds' and enter your bank details for auto-debit. A verification SMS check is needed. 2. For each fund, search the AMFI code given in this plan (e.g., 147622 for Motilal Oswal). Select the 'Growth' option (where gains are reinvested, not paid out as cash). 3. Set up monthly SIPs for ₹5,400 in the mid-cap fund, ₹8,100 in Flexi cap fund, ₹8,100 in Large-&-Mid cap fund, and ₹5,400 in the Liquid Fund. 4. Attach your savings account as the source for all SIPs. Test-run with ₹100 for one month to confirm everything works, then switch to full amounts. 5. Future-proof: If you ever get extra money as a bonus, you can invest that as a lump-sum, but your base plan is purely SIP-only.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Open your app and verify all 4 SIPs are auto-deducting. If you're using Groww, check that none of the SIPs show a 'Failed' or 'Transaction Pending' status. 2. After 18 months: If any one fund has underperformed its own benchmark (e.g., Nifty Midcap 150 Index) by more than 8-10% over 2 years, pick another fund from the same category. But don't rush – let it play unless there's a glaring issue. 3. Never review during the first 12 months. Set a reminder on your phone to review this same plan only once every year on the same date.

Once a year, on the anniversary of your first investment, check the allocation percentages in your app: 1. If 'Midcap' has grown to 25% instead of the planned 20%, sell just enough Midcap units to bring it back to 20% and add that money to Flexi/Large-Mid. This simple act smooths out highs and lows over decades.
plan.rationale: At 35 with ₹6.1L already saved, you're in the best position to build wealth aggressively. With a 32-year horizon, even a small edge in returns compounds massively. Since you're in the new tax regime (no 80C), we skip tax-saving ELSS funds and focus purely on low-cost index/active hybrids. 25% of your ₹27k/month is parked in a safe debt fund now to reduce volatility. Over 32 years, this setup can grow to over ₹3.5 crore, giving you enough cushion for a house upgrade, your child's education (₹20L), and creating that long-term passive income stream.
risks: Equity markets can drop 30-40% in a bad year – your balance sheet may go from ₹50L to ₹30L temporarily. But with 32 years ahead, time is your friend. Historical data shows Indian stocks recover within 3-5 years most of the time. 2. Inflation can erode purchasing power over decades; this plan targets ~14% CAGR to outpace inflation by 3-5%. 3. If your risk appetite has lowered due to life events (e.g., income loss, health crisis), review the asset mix – lower equity exposure is safer.
For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.
Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
The plan scores approximately 0.6 on the APS spectrum.

The equity sleeve (70% of the portfolio) includes one genuine passive index fund (20%), but the other 50% of the equity allocation is split between two active funds (Flexi Cap and Large-Mid), both explicitly described as capitalizing on active manager stock-picking abilities. Therefore, passive_instrument_fraction is 0.4 (20% passive / 70% total equity).

The strategy is highly buy-and-hold oriented with annual rebalancing only and a strict rule to avoid reviews for the first 12 months, resulting in a high turnover_score of 0.9. The time horizon is exceptionally long (32 years), and the plan warns against giving in to short-term market corrections, earning a near-perfect time_horizon_alignment_score.

While the plan mentions 'low-cost' and avoiding ELSS due to tax regime, it does not explicitly make expense ratio the primary selection criterion, leading to a moderate cost_emphasis_score of 0.4.

The rationale and descriptions focus on manager experience and stock-picking mandates rather than deep individual stock analysis, suggesting a score around 0.7 for research_vs_cost.

Portfolio activeness is moderate (0.6) because the specific quantitative metrics (alpha, tracking error, beta) were not provided for the funds. The presence of one index fund and two funds with explicit 'picking' mandates implies a mix of active and passive risk profiles, lacking the distinct high-tracking-error data needed to confirm genuine alpha generation as per the instructions.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment is strong (aggressive stance, 32-year horizon, SIP preference) but slightly imperfect as the plan does not explicitly map funds to specific goals (house, education) and the 20% cash allocation seems arbitrary for a 32-year horizon. Diversification is weak: the portfolio lacks global diversification, sector diversification, and bond diversification. It is over-exposed to Indian equity (70% in three overlapping funds) and lacks small-cap, gold, or international exposure. Internal consistency is high: allocations, SIP amounts, and rationales are coherent and mathematically consistent. Tax efficiency is excellent for a New Regime investor: the plan correctly avoids ELSS (unable to use 80C) and uses equity-wrapped instruments to enjoy preferential LTCG tax. Risk-return appropriateness is high: the high equity allocation matches an aggressive appetite, and the plan acknowledges downside scenarios and inflation, though the 20% debt buffer is a bit low for typical aggressive portfolios, which still fits the very long horizon.
